video_title: Why Wall Street's Biggest Traders Are Abandoning Crypto for Prediction Markets | Alex Momot
transcript: Just imagine I'm as a regular trader I'm trying to do something on Polymarket and once I just misclicked my order instead of doing limit order I made a market order and just $1,000 could change the odds for 1.5%. So very thin order book it means that it's really hard for the regular user to trade. >> Here we are with Alex Momot the co-founder of Peanut Trade which focuses on making for prediction markets. Alex, welcome to the Defined. It's great to have you. >> Hello Camilla, thank you very much. >> Alex, can we just start by understanding in in basic terms what Peanut Trade does? >> Yeah, so actually Peanut Trade is a market maker both on regular web three markets and prediction markets as well. So we are doing a lot of stuff. We started as a AMV searcher back in 2020 with the DeFi summer and now continuing through several bear markets till 2026 when we have a quite a new trends with prediction markets with AI, with a lot of new areas and the technologies that changing our landscape quite fast. >> Why why focus on on prediction markets? >> First of all I was Ethereum ICO participants and it was one of great idea that I heard from Vitalik Buterin back in 2014 and 15. So early days of Ethereum and I didn't understood in full matter the concept at that time. I think that smart contract contracts was a a little bit better concept to understand at the beginning. But I remember that idea of predicting something through distributed technology even if it's not fully distributed now it is more like centralized platforms. You know Kalshi and Polymarket as leaders are the leaders here but overall the idea is great and I think that the geopolitical and other events type, not only sport betting, is something that could be the next big thing in terms of hedging and many other usage for not always, but something that could help us to push several technologies including tokenization because I don't think tokenization will be able to succeed in the nearest way because of legal because of problems in each particular market, but with prediction markets with all of the technologies around that, I think it will give us significant boost including agents from AI including some other things that together can give a significant technology development with a very simple idea as a fundamentals. And that's you know, that's ideology behind the answer. And another thing, it's money because we all know that the bear market in cryptocurrency wiping out a lot of companies. It's a significant problem for many projects and we can see that in opposite to that prediction markets doing quite well and I think that it will continue. So, we are trying to keep up with the trend. >> Thanks. So, the the market for for prediction market is becoming increasingly more sophisticated. Recently, Wintermute also said that they will start market making for these platforms. So, what does this signal mean? The fact that there there is more interest in in market making at and prediction markets. Does it mean that these markets are kind of coming out of their more experimental phase and becoming more institutional? >> I will not say it's becoming more institutional in that way, but just from the money perspective, we know that revenue of I believe Jane Street last year was around $40 billion dollars, and we know some internal figures about other market makers of the same size. We know that only 3% of their revenue came from crypto. And this desire for the like classic Web 3 market maker to expand on something new, including prediction markets and TradFi by the way as well, it means so revenues decline in regular Web 3 market making quite heavily. Uh we can see that because we are competing with with Wintermute and other market makers in many areas. And if some things like MEV during the day, maybe several months ago it was 20K per day in profits for MEV bots. It means that it's significant decline from, I don't know, several years ago. And it means that for such a big companies with giant resources, with a lot of volumes, it is obvious that they will try to expand in some newer areas. So in it's only about the profits. And I can say that Wintermute delay their expense, I would say, because just maybe 6 months ago or 1 year ago when we started our experiments, they were still in Web 3 market. But I think that we will have new players quite soon. Everybody who have quite, you know, significant experience in development of strategies will try to do something in predictions. >> What's the the impact of there being more market makers in prediction markets? >> I think that very important is with a smaller volume it could be a larger profits. So margin much higher. Another thing is that it is little bit more easy to compete with big companies like Jane Street, like Susquehanna Group, like Jump. Because something very new is not so complicated and you don't need a lot of money, a lot of infrastructure, with very high speed. So, like level of competition for TradFi is much higher. Level of competition on Web3 markets a little bit lower. And for prediction markets, you can try to do something very simple, but with much higher results. And that's why everybody trying to step up and try to extract profits same like it was 6 years ago on during the DeFi summer at the early days of any year. >> What about for the end user? What's the impact for for the user of of prediction markets? >> That's definitely a good thing for the user because just imagine I'm as a regular trader, I'm trying to do something on Polymarket. And once I just misclicked my order instead of doing limit order, I made a market order. And just $1,000 could change the odds for 1.5%. So, a very thin order book, it means that it's really hard for the regular user to trade. So, $1,000 for regular crypto markets is just nothing. It could be easily absorbed by the regular market maker, even if it's some kind of I don't know, tier three exchange from China. But even for Polymarket, it's quite a significant volume for something connected with Iran. So, it's a popular topic, but still it's impossible to trade. And I hope that it will be more liquidity in the order books. That's actually what we are doing right now, helping projects to put liquidity inside. And in my humble opinion, you should start if you are building prediction market, you should start only with the market maker because otherwise, it will be impossible not only to buy, but quite often it's impossible to exit from your position. So, definitely market making and liquidity issue is something that will be a hot topic and a very important thing for the development of prediction. >> Definitely. And um is Pynk Trade market making in uh Polymarket and and Kalshi? >> Yeah, so we using Polymarket and Kalshi as our source of liquidity for the small platforms. If we divide all prediction market world into two parts, we have currently the origination of alpha, we can say, so the original price. Now, more on Kalshi, I would say. Kalshi is more institutional, very often higher volume, and Polymarket is more like a crypto-friendly, but sometimes it is only second after Kalshi. But, what is more important to make a market maker on that platforms, you should have a very unique understanding of fair price, and for that we building a very special strategies. I will not say something in particular, because probably we have a race here between very big companies with much bigger resources than we do, and because of that, it's really interesting how it will become maybe in several monthses or in several years, but I can say that in according to our internal research, Polymarket and Kalshi outperforming some payment, how to say, some providers of data about sports book or something like that, and they are taking a lot of money for that. So, actually, prediction markets currently much better source for the actual data about something that's happening in the world than some kind of old players that became obsolete because of that. Because Polymarket and Kalshi, that's a place where a lot of researchers can put their ideas and try to earn money because of that. And actually, it is working much better than very big professional companies. And I think that's great, and that's exactly what was in pitch of Vitalik Buterin back in 10 years ago. >> Yeah, we we we have seen how how these markets have become more accurate than many polling firms and so on. And I I think that's because of this just like greater liquidity, but it's also because of this more controversial topic, which is the incentive to do insider trading since now, you know, anyone with privileged information can profit off it from these prediction markets. So, what's your view there? >> So, I think that probably insider trading problem is much more visible on predictions because of specific of the market. So, it's still quite widespread on regular web 3 platforms, but it is just, you know, something that everybody just stick to and everybody understands that it could happen. And the level of understanding and level of how to say that everybody just knows that it's a normal situation for web 3. Of course, it's not a normal, but because of some traditional legacy, it's already like, you know, something that was us for many years. And for predictions, it is much more transparent because if you know that some guy that flying together with American SWAT to Venezuela will make a bet and then FBI will do a research or, I don't know, some internal special service will check that and we will know about that. That's not because it's a unique problem of prediction markets. It's just probably problem of liquidity as well because very thin order books and ability to be visible and transparency because of crypto creates it much a bigger problem. It's, you know, something like banks told us about crypto that crypto is something gray, something bad, and then we can transparently see every transaction once done, and we cannot do that with banks anyway, anyhow, and that's why it's not a proper approach to that question. I think that prediction market insider trading problem is something that will be fixed quite often with some internal policies. And just recently, we know a lot about some bans of the accounts, and I think it will be just a matter of several years how to establish proper rules, and problem will not imply heavily on the succession of this concept. It It will be just another thing that should be solved, and I'm sure that it will be solved. >> That's It's fair enough. That's true that um the transparency provided by these platforms is a way to actually detect insider trading in a way that traditional markets uh that it's it's it's harder to do in traditional markets. So, you you you mentioned how Tina trades gets liquidity from Kalshi and Polymarket and and uses that liquidity in in smaller prediction markets. Can you Can you further explain how that works? >> Yeah, so actually it's quite a simple concept. If you're talking about something some other platform, uh we can be a maker on that other platform, and we can be a taker on Polymarket and Kalshi. And actually we are using our own liquidity for that. But it is, you know, very first stage of very simple homogeny market. So, if it's absolutely similar to the market on Qual- Polymarket and Kalshi, it will work like that. If it's a little bit different, we need a different pricing model. It is really hard to understand what is the price and alpha, and how we can take a proper price somewhere out of existing prediction market space. A very simple explanation, if you will think about the price origination of Bitcoin, it's definitely not Polymarket and Kalshi for now. Maybe it will change, and maybe Polymarket and Kalshi could surpass volumes and liquidity of Binance. I don't know. For For now, it's not. For now, we can use Coinbase, Binance, Daxes, and many other sources to combine and understand the real price origination and then use it on the prediction market size. Same you can have on sport betting on some geopolitical events and it means like a second level of market making on predictions. For all that biggest companies that I mentioned, they using very complicated models. They have a lot of people. They have quite a good infrastructure and it works outside the predictions. But I would say that in the order books we have competition between very big companies and some smaller prey players. Sometimes it's just a small prop trading companies or even individual traders that could predict something actually better than anybody else. And that's the essence of prediction markets. That's the old beauty of the idea is that some small guy with a very small resources currently could be could have same success on the same level like a super giant Chicago boys with a lot of money and significant number of employees. >> So but what about for the the the the the more niche markets? Like okay, Bitcoin price is easy to maybe consolidate markets across different platforms. But for something like I don't know, this like controversial market that recently closed. Did Michael Saylor will Michael Saylor sell Bitcoin in May? You know, that's it's There was definitely a gray area. There are different rules on like how that's defined and and maybe different parameters across platforms. So how how can you consolidate liquidity across markets that aren't identical? >> Yeah, so first of all, that's a very simple concept to compare markets if it's homogeneous or not. We have a special AI tool that doing that for us and sometimes it's hard for me even if I will try to understand all of the rules. For my personal trading, I lost a lot of money because I didn't check the rules. I know it for myself. And I think that's something that AI could could help us with. So, it's quite obvious that you should just check all of the rules with AI, and then you will receive exact explanation how you should compare those markets or what will be the outcome, is it a similar or not. We have a lot of very similar markets by name, but not the similar by rules. And of course, for fun, you can use this markets just to make a bets and share with friends. But if you're doing in a professional way, if you want to be a successful trader, you should analyze it. And I'm sure that we will have absolutely different approach. We currently building that, you know, aggregated liquidity core that will use all of the available prediction markets, compare markets, try to merge similar liquidity across different venues. And then we will have AI tool that will help us. It will be inbuilt in the platform. So, people will be able to understand that AI agent will update their position according to actual rules. And definitely, it will cause some changes because UMA voting on Polymarket sometimes quite strange for many users. And overall oracle problem and niche markets, it's something very subjective. And it's really hard, but you know, it's same hard like our interaction with any LLM model because we have our own context. And for AI, it could be some different approach. So, that's something that we will need to spend time a little bit more to stand how we do properly. But my belief that because of AI, all that could be solved quite easily, and I'm sure that it's no need to create like thousands of markets. I think that it will be indexes that will combine very hot topics in just one index. Let's say some kind of escalation in Middle East or Trump economy thoughts or something like that. It will be just one big index, and it will be much easier to interact with it. >> Interesting. And And in that case, when if everything is consolidating in big markets, um is there How How likely is it that a small, new upstart or entrant into the prediction market world can can succeed? It It It seems like it's, you know, these two giants. Can Can smaller prediction markets start up, succeed in this environment? >> Yeah, so you know, it's um same issue for any kind of industry. If you have two very big players, it's always impossible for them to use all of the niche. They trying to expand on some other things, but they will never be able to be as flexible as needed for some particular case. And we can see same. So, uh probably several years ago, one of Binance associate told me something like it was quite easy when we was quite small. So, we just tried to see in front of us and going straight forward. And now, because we are leader of the space, we should go back and we should check what is behind. So, we can adjust and adopt and learn from our smaller competitors. And I think it will be same problem for Polymarket and Cult because 20 billion valuation for each of them is not something that could be justified because of just increasing numbers of volume. There are many other aspects around legal, uh around how really popular prediction markets in some how this popular in some niche is it only a sport books is it geopolitical events or some cultural events. So, I think that definitely it's still niche for a lot of other approaches. The only important thing for that is that of course you should be prepared for your main launch. So, if it's a testnet it's okay. If it's some closed better it's okay, but if you're going live you should understand that you will have a liquidity at the start that something that market maker should help you with and many of our clients waiting for for the release until we will fully integrate and deploy our capital to start working because without that for many users it will be impossible to trade at all and that's kind of chicken and egg problem, you know, when Uber or other taxi app started many years ago they should create some Fleet before entering the space. Otherwise it will be no orders from the user with no cars nearby. So, same here it should be chicken egg problem, but if you solve that you have a very unique vision and you have some kind of differentiation. I think it's still possible and definitely that kind of companies should be bigger. So, it's not normal situation when we have 20 billion companies and just a lot of smaller players. I think that it will be some mid-size companies and it will be quite popular as well just a little bit differentiated. >> Speaking of bigger players, Hyperliquid is also may starting to interact or compete in this area with with its new ATP for markets that you know, allow traders to build their own markets on based on Hyperliquid. How how does that compete with >> Well, they started quite efficiently I would say. So, they introduced zero commissions and it's another significant boost because it's possible to create own markets if you have a stake on the HYP token. And because of that, some very good companies like prop funds could create their own markets. So, that's definitely a kind of example for smaller players how to enter the space. And I think that approach of Hyperliquid is much better than approach of Polymarket and Kalshi in opening some kind of futures trading. So, it's, you know, opposite entering the space. So, they're trying to conquer each one space and I don't think that it's quite successful for the prediction market size. It's just, you know, just a regular, okay, so we will introduce same markets without any kind of differentiation. And Hyperliquid tried a little bit smarter and their team quite, you know, thinking out of the box and definitely that's very interesting approach. I don't know will it work as planned. So, maybe, you know, sometimes it's a network effect for the bigger players and for Polymarket and Kalshi it's quite easy to use their traders and maybe it's a little bit different positioning because Polymarket and Kalshi is that something more for retail and Hyperliquid it is more from for a bigger size. My bet is that the size of regular trade on Hyperliquid much bigger. And because of that it's it will be different trader. But let's see, competition it's always great for us and probably each particular user will be on a good side with win because of bigger competition. >> Definitely. And speaking of that, how does Phenom Trade compete with the the big market makers entering the space that that we discussed. >> So, we adjusted our offer. I understand that Jane Street and Susquehanna will not work with some smaller companies if they just raise their small round, they will not be able to provide volumes and of course their integration cost and other costs that they need to carry to start working, it's not worth it and they will not wait until millions will come. And the our model here is same. We have a little bit different with biggest but same with web three market making for retainers. So, we introduced retainer model until smaller players will have their own rebate program. So, no need to prepare some kind of rebates until you will have volumes and it's kind of, you know, rent of our algorithm. So, we can ensure that smaller player will be able to spend necessary time to build their marketing, to try and they will have liquidity from the day one. On the opposite side, we have a giant companies, you know, if uh Jane Street earned $40 billion during one year. So, of course it will be really hard for some small startup to invite them to work on something about I don't know, will some singer do something stupid in their Twitter. So, it's not the things that could be interested for such a giant companies. And we are closing that gap and we are expanding into very specific uh markets. We can even Now, we understand that because we have a lot of partners from predictions. We know a lot about the actual launch of prediction markets and now we have a separate service consulting for the projects, how to enter the space, what to do, how big could be a delay, how fast you should be, how unique should be your markets or it is better to do homogeneous markets with a little bit different name from Kalshi market. So, a lot of data that we can share and that will help them to make a bootstrap and faster start overall. >> Awesome. Which where do you think there's a there's opportunity in in the prediction market space? >> I think the main opportunity is increasing comfort of trading for the larger amounts. So, on our side what we are trying to achieve and we are analyzing the median size of the trade and we adjust in our liquidity. So, if it's a little bit higher than it was expected before and we will adjust order book accordingly. So, your typical user from each prediction market will be able to trade with a less slippage and the slippage overall and the low liquidity is that's a super giant problem for prediction markets and for me it's a significant advantage for us because you know, it's like from 300 Spartans. It's a very small place. So, you don't need billion dollars for that. We can enter this space and compete with these big figures just because we can be exactly where we needed to be and because of other sophisticated instruments how we can achieve that. And that's something that should be prepared for each particular market and I think that that's advantage of a smaller companies not from the side of prediction markets, but from the market making and other service providers. >> Makes sense. And then to wrap up, what's next for for Peanut Trade? Like what are some of the exciting features or road map milestones that you're working towards? >> Yes, so actually I think that our core of liquidity provision in the prediction markets will be as the unique tool for trading and kind of prime brokerage or something like that. Actually, that's a different model, but it is more understandable for the wide audience that we will have a original alpha, we will have a better price, we will be able to use it for both for our clients. Maybe we'll have retail users on it. I still think that we need more liquidity and then it will be much more popular for B2B segment and a lot of people and specifically AI agents will be able to hedge everyone's risk. So, if you have any particular exposure, if it's connected with your geography, if you live in Dubai, for example, you have a exposure to risk of escalation in the Middle East. So, you should have ability to hedge the that risk with the prediction markets and your AI agent could do it for you. And I hope that it will be a super cool solution for everybody. It will work under the hood. So, your personal AI agent will solve everything. You will not need to spend a lot of time checking the rules, trying to aggregate everything, checking slippage, how big could be the trade. Everything will be done um automated with AI agents and that's a good thing and that's a proper use of both AI and prediction markets technology. >> Awesome. That's exciting to see. And Alex, it was it was great to learn more about Finate Trade and really great insights about prediction markets more broadly. So, thank you so much for joining me and we'll be following the Finate Trade journey. >> Thank you. Thank you, Camilla. Bye. >> Thank you, Camilla. Have a good
